front: Define cut-vertex (articulation point) in a graph
back: A cut-vertex is a vertex whose removal (along with all edges incident to it) disconnects the graph or increases the number of connected components. It represents a critical point in network structure.

front: Define cut-set (edge cut) in a graph
back: A cut-set is a set of edges whose removal disconnects the graph (increases number of connected components). A minimal cut-set (bridge) cannot have any proper subset that is also a cut-set.

front: What is a bridge in graph theory?
back: A bridge (or cut-edge) is an edge whose removal disconnects the graph. It is a cut-set of size 1. Every bridge in a connected graph is necessarily a cut-edge.
A biconnected (2-connected) graph is a connected graph that remains connected after removal of any single vertex. It has no cut-vertices.

front: What is a block in graph theory?
back: A block is a maximal biconnected subgraph. Any edge in a block belongs to a single block. Blocks are connected by cut-vertices.
Vertex connectivity κ(G) is the minimum number of vertices whose removal disconnects the graph or reduces it to a trivial graph. For complete graphs, κ(Kn) = n-1.
Edge connectivity λ(G) is the minimum number of edges whose removal disconnects the graph. It equals the minimum degree δ(G) for regular graphs.

front: State the condition for a vertex to be an articulation point in DFS tree
back: A vertex v (not root) is an articulation point if it has a child u such that no vertex in the subtree of u has a back edge to an ancestor of v. Root is articulation if it has ≥2 children in DFS tree.
A separating pair (or vertex cut) is a set of vertices whose removal disconnects the graph. A k-vertex cut contains k vertices.
Ear decomposition is a way to construct 2-connected graphs by starting with a cycle and adding paths (ears) whose endpoints are already in the graph but internal vertices are new.
A block-cut tree is a bipartite tree where one partition contains cut-vertices and the other contains blocks. Edges connect cut-vertices to their respective blocks.

front: Menger's theorem states that the minimum number of vertices separating two vertices equals the maximum number of internally vertex-disjoint paths between them.
Define trivial graph and its connectivity
back: A trivial graph has one vertex. Its vertex connectivity is 0 (or defined as ∞ in some contexts for complete graphs). A graph with no cut-vertices is 2-connected.

front: How does removal of a cut-vertex affect blocks?
back: When a cut-vertex is removed, the graph may split into multiple components. Each block that was connected through that cut-vertex becomes separate.

front: What is the relationship between bridges and cut-vertices?
back: If an edge is a bridge, at least one of its endpoints is a cut-vertex (unless the graph has only 2 vertices). However, a cut-vertex may exist without any bridge incident to it.
